The problem involves two dormitories named Oakwood and Maplewood.
We know that the total number of students in both dorms is 96.
A relocation event occurred where five students moved from Maplewood to Oakwood.
After this move, the number of residents in both dormitories became equal.
This means each dormitory now holds exactly half of the total population.
Half of ninety-six students is forty-eight students per dormitory.
So, after the move, Oakwood has forty-eight students inside.
We need to find out how many students were in Oakwood before the move.
Since five students arrived at Oakwood to reach that number of forty-eight, we must subtract them.
Taking forty-eight and subtracting five gives us the original count for Oakwood.
The calculation shows that Oakwood originally had forty-three students.
We can also check the numbers for Maplewood to ensure consistency.
If Oakwood had forty-three, then Maplewood must have had the rest of the ninety-six.
Subtracting forty-three from ninety-six leaves fifty-three students in Maplewood initially.
When five students leave Maplewood, the count drops from fifty-three to forty-eight.
This matches the count in Oakwood after the five students arrive.
Both dorms indeed have forty-eight students after the relocation process.
The math confirms that the initial number for Oakwood was forty-three.
